title: Declare an empty slice
instruction: A slice is like an array but with no fixed size — the brackets are empty. Run this to see an empty slice ready to receive books.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	var books []string
	var pages []int
	fmt.Println(books)
	fmt.Println(pages)
	fmt.Println(len(books))
	fmt.Println(len(pages))
}
successMessage: Empty slices print as []. Length is 0. No panic, just ready and waiting.

title: Declare with values
instruction: `books := []string{"Dune", "1984", "Moby Dick"}` — no size number needed. Go counts for you. Create an `authors` slice with 4 names and print it.

codeChecks.message: Create an authors slice: authors := []string{...}
Print it: fmt.Println(authors)
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: []type{values} — no size needed. Go counts for you.

title: append — add to the end
instruction: `books = append(books, "Neuromancer")` adds a book. Critical: you must reassign the result. Append three books one at a time, printing after each.
expectedOutput: [Dune 1984 Moby Dick Neuromancer Fahrenheit 451 Brave New World]

codeChecks.message: Append Neuromancer: books = append(books, "Neuromancer")
Append Fahrenheit 451: books = append(books, "Fahrenheit 451")
Append Brave New World: books = append(books, "Brave New World")
Replace the TODO lines with your append calls.

successMessage: append returns a new slice — always reassign. That's the one rule beginners forget.

title: len and cap 🤯
instruction: Every slice has a length (`len`) and a capacity (`cap`). When you append and run out of space, Go doubles the capacity. Run this to watch them change.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	books := []string{}
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(books), cap(books))
	books = append(books, "Dune")
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(books), cap(books))
	books = append(books, "1984")
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(books), cap(books))
	books = append(books, "Moby Dick")
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(books), cap(books))
	books = append(books, "Neuromancer")
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(books), cap(books))
	books = append(books, "Fahrenheit 451")
	fmt.Printf("len=%d cap=%d\n", len(books), cap(books))
}
successMessage: When the slice fills up, Go doubles the capacity. len is what you have. cap is what Go reserved.

title: Access and update by index
instruction: Slices use the same zero-indexed syntax as arrays. A patron returned a damaged book — replace the book at index 2 with "The Great Gatsby".

codeChecks.message: Update index 2: books[2] = "The Great Gatsby"
Replace the TODO comment with your assignment.

successMessage: Reading and writing by index works exactly like arrays. The flexible part is the size.

title: Loop with range
instruction: `for i, v := range books` gives you index and value. Loop through and print each as `"Book 1: Dune"`, `"Book 2: 1984"`, etc.
hint: for i, v := range books { fmt.Printf("Book %d: %s\n", i+1, v) }

codeChecks.message: Use range to loop: for i, v := range books
Use i+1 so the number starts at 1 not 0.
Replace the TODO comment with your loop.

successMessage: Same range syntax as arrays. Slices just don't have a fixed size.

title: Slicing a slice
instruction: `books[1:3]` gives you indexes 1 and 2 (high end is exclusive). Print just the middle three books from the collection.

codeChecks.message: Print the middle three with books[1:4] — index 1, 2, and 3.
Replace the TODO comment with your slice expression.

successMessage: Low is inclusive, high is exclusive. That rule applies everywhere in Go.

title: copy() — the real duplicate 🤯
instruction: Here's a trap: `b := a` does NOT copy a slice. Both point to the same data. Use `copy(dst, src)` for a genuine separate copy. Fix the code so changing b doesn't change a.
hint: b := make([]string, len(a)) then copy(b, a) — two lines replace the one assignment.

codeChecks.message: Create b with make: b := make([]string, len(a))
Copy a into b: copy(b, a)

successMessage: b := a shares the data. copy() separates it. This is one of the most important slice concepts in Go.

title: Build a library catalog
instruction: Start with 4 books, append "Brave New World", then print a numbered catalog from 1 to 5.
hint: Append, then range loop with i+1.

codeChecks.message: Append the new book: books = append(books, "Brave New World")
Use range to loop through books.
Use i+1 so the numbering starts at 1.
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: A working library catalog. You created it, added to it, and printed it cleanly.
